planting.note: Root tip cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, poor to average soil in full sun; za'atar demands perfect drainage and full sun — it rots quickly in wet or poorly drained conditions.

directions: Root tip cuttings in moist perlite or plant container specimens in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, poor to average soil in full sun; za'atar is the traditional herb of the Middle East whose leaves and flowers constitute the primary ingredient of the za'atar spice blend used throughout Lebanese, Syrian, Palestinian, Israeli, and Turkish cooking. The plant is distinctly more aromatic, savory, and intense in flavor than common oregano (O. vulgare), with a complex savory-thyme-oregano scent that is characteristic of the regional cuisine. Harvest stems before flowering for the most intense flavor. The dried herb is mixed with sesame seeds, sumac, and olive oil to create the classic za'atar condiment. In zones below 8, grow in a container brought indoors in winter. Extremely drought-tolerant and deer-resistant. Very similar to and sometimes confused with the closely related O. onites (Cretan oregano).

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in well-drained perlite or plant container-grown specimens in sharply drained, alkaline to neutral, poor to average soil in full sun; woolly thyme tolerates foot traffic and drought exceptionally well once established and is ideal for filling gaps between paving stones.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in sharply drained, alkaline to neutral, poor to average soil in full sun; woolly thyme forms one of the lowest, densest, most carpet-like groundcovers available, producing a silvery-gray, velvet-textured mat of tiny, densely woolly leaves only 1–3 inches tall that spreads steadily without becoming aggressive. Pale pink flowers cover the mat in early summer, attracting bees. The plant is one of very few groundcovers that tolerates moderate foot traffic and thrives between flagstones, along dry stone paths, and in gravel gardens. It is primarily ornamental — the woolly leaves are not as flavorful for cooking as other thymes — and is grown for its exceptional textural effect. Excellent in rock gardens, as an alternative to lawn in dry, sunny spots, and in Mediterranean-style plantings.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 4–6 weeks early in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Korean mint self-sows freely and often maintains a persistent colony by self-seeding in suitable conditions.

directions: Direct sow or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Korean mint (Korean hyssop) is an East Asian culinary and medicinal herb with large, anise-mint-scented leaves used fresh in Korean, Vietnamese, and Chinese cooking — particularly in ssam (lettuce wrap), salads, and teas — and dense spikes of lavender-purple to pink flowers from midsummer through fall that attract extraordinary numbers of bees and swallowtail butterflies. Distinctly different from the North American native A. foeniculum (anise hyssop) in flavor and growing conditions, though botanically similar. More heat-tolerant and easier in southern gardens. Self-sows freely once established; leave some spent flower stalks for seed to maintain the colony.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; calamint is exceptionally drought-tolerant once established and thrives in conditions that challenge most herbs.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; calamint (nepitella) is a Mediterranean and European herb with small, rounded, intensely minty-oregano-scented leaves and a sustained cloud of tiny, lavender-blue to white tubular flowers from midsummer through frost that attract bees in extraordinary numbers — few plants are visited more intensely or continuously by native bees at peak bloom. The plant forms a neat, mounding, drought-resistant groundcover that is virtually pest-free. Used in Italian cooking as a substitute for mint in pasta and bean dishes (the leaves are more savory and less sweet than true mint). Self-sows moderately in suitable conditions. Excellent for edging, rock gardens, and sunny herb borders. 'Blue Cloud' (compact, intense blue) is a widely grown cultivar.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; baby sage blooms most heavily from summer through fall, and a hard cut-back in late winter promotes vigorous new growth.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; baby sage is a compact, woody-based subshrub native to Mexico and Arizona that is one of the finest ornamental sages for Gulf Coast and Southwest gardens, producing small, blackcurrant-scented leaves and racemes of two-lipped flowers in brilliant cherry-red, ruby, pink, or the famous bicolor pattern of 'Hot Lips' (red upper lip, white lower lip that reverts to pure red in heat). Blooms nearly year-round in mild climates, from late spring through frost. Hummingbirds visit the flowers constantly. Cut back to 6–12 inches above ground in late winter to promote vigorous new growth. Extremely drought-resistant once established. 'Hot Lips' is the most widely grown selection; 'Cerro Potosi' (true cherry-red) is a classic.
